title: Lesson 415
transcript: This next section deals with process improvement, right? We've been talking about software development in general in this domain here. One of the goals that any organization should have is to continually improve their software development processes. So there are two maturity models that are specifically mentioned in the exam outline, and we're going to cover those in this section here. And again, the whole purpose of maturity models as you see here, is to measure an organization's maturity in specific processes with the goal of continuous process improvement. These models allow you to look at how mature you are in certain processes within the organization, for the goal of improving them and making them better continuously. So these are focused obviously on software development processes. Although I think when you look at, for example, CMMI, which is the first one that we're gonna talk about here, and that obviously stands for what you see here. capability maturity model integration. This was developed by the Software Engineering Institute specifically for software development. but there are versions of it that can be applied for other processes in the organization, right? And the idea behind this is that you look at your process and you understand how mature you are and it uses a scale of one to five, as you can see here. The idea being you understand how mature you are in a specific process, but then the whole goal is to allow the organization to improve, to get to a level of five, which is the desired level. It's the optimizing level here where you've got processes that get better each and every time and things finish on time, on budget and resources are able to deal with things in a proactive way as opposed to being able to react. So the idea is continuous process improvement. And so this again, as I said earlier, was developed by the Software Engineering Institute. to be able to measure how good you are in software development for the purpose of getting better. And as far as what the exam really requires, first of all is to understand the scale that this uses. So one, for example, is not very good. In fact, there's even a zero level that says you don't really know the process because you've never done it before. But the idea of one is that it's not very good, right? you're using some sort of process that is unpredictable, you're doing it for the very first time, for example, and problems are gonna occur and so on. So that's the initial phase, which is one. Two is referred to as being managed, right? So now you have more manage capabilities within that process it's still reactive problems still occur and it does impact timelines and budgets and so on three is where you start getting proactive right in fact the delineation between reactive and proactive happens between levels two and three, as you can see here. And then when you start getting, for example, to level four, you have really good capabilities where you have project management capabilities and proactive ways to deal with problems that does not impact. your timelines and deadlines and so on. And again, the goal here is to try and get to level five, which is referred to as optimizing, where every time you do that process, it gets even better and you finish under budget and under timelines and so on. really good way and as I said earlier this was developed for obviously software development but organizations have taken the same sort of concept and applied it to other processes in the organization from a management perspective it makes total sense to be able to apply something like this right because the whole process the whole purpose of this is to continually improve your processes so that they get better over time so understanding where you are with the purpose of getting better is something that organizations want not only in software development, but elsewhere in all kinds of processes. But this again was developed for specifically software development by the software engineering Institute. Now what we've seen as far as the exam is concerned is remembering the five levels and the one word description that you see here that describes each one of them. So for example, one is initial, two is managed, three is defined. Four is quantitatively managed. And five, which is the desired level that you ultimately want to get to, is optimizing. And the wording that we've put above here sort of describes each of those. But what's really important, and we've seen people get questions related to this, is your processes are actually tailored or start becoming tailored at level three, all right? So they're tailored towards, for example, continuous improvement. And level four is where you start understanding your processes and they're very predictable. What's going to happen is very predictable. All right. So associate tailored with level three and predictable with level four as well as far as the capability maturity model integration. maturity model is all about. Now, the other one that is also mentioned in the exam outline is something that's been put out by OWASP. And you'll probably remember who OWASP is. It's the Open Web Application Security Project. And they're most well known for the top 10 that they regularly publish on a regular basis. For example, the top 10 vulnerabilities and exploits associated with, for example, web applications. And they also publish a mobile top 10 vulnerabilities and so on on a regular basis. But OWASP has a wealth of all kinds of things that can be used by organizations and resources for example that are useful especially in applications development and software development and one of the things that has gone through you know iterations of improvement is what you see here it's called SAM which obviously stands for software assurance maturity model so it's another model just like the one that we just talked about CMMI which is focused on understanding how mature you are in your software development processes for the whole purpose of getting better. And the way that this one works is that it's got 15 security practices that you see within the boxes here, right? But they're all structured in five business processes that you see up here like for example governance design implementation verification and operations Those are the corporate business processes and each of those will have their own security practices, these 15 that you see underneath them. I want to make sure that I get this right so that you know everything that there is to know as far as the exam is concerned. I'm going to give you a little more information here. Again, the whole purpose of any of these, including CMMI and SAM that you see here, I think we're in version 2 of SAM, but it provides a means of knowing where your organization actually is on its journey towards, for example, software development and software assurance, so that ultimately we can produce very secure and functional software, but it looks at your processes to be able to do that. The whole idea here is to be able to move to the next level, for example, so that you can mature, you can get better over time. And as I just said, it's structured in 15 security practices that are grouped in five business functions that you see at the top here. Every security practice contains a set of activities, as you can see. structured into three maturity levels. So each of these 15 security practices have a scale of one to three as far as maturity levels are concerned. The activities on a lower maturity level are typically easier to execute. and require less formalization than the ones on a higher maturity level so the idea is you can measure your maturity level in each of these 15 security practices so that you ultimately can get to level three of each of these ones to get ultimately better Security practices, as you can see here, have activities grouped into logical flows and divided into two streams. That's what these little stream colors that you see here. represent streams, cover different aspects of a practice, and have their own objectives, aligning and linking the activities in the practice over the different maturity levels. And for each security practice, this model defines three maturity levels as we said each level has a successfully more sophisticated objective with specific activities and more strict success metrics so one of the things that this also emphasizes are metrics that you can provide obviously to management to be able to understand Thank you. how mature you are, not only overall, for example, in your software development, but in each of these security practices that you see here that are typically very important, right? I mean, look, you look at certain activities here that focus on things like secure deployment and testing and management and so on, all important things that you would address security in any sort of important corporate governance activity, including software development and how it relates to obviously these very important business functions that you see up here like governance, design, implementation and so on. So a really good way to relate software development to business activities and how security fits in is what this provides.
